channel.title: QE ArXiv Watch Weekly
channel.description: Weekly AI-synthesized digest of quality engineering research from arXiv. Covering Control Charts, Experimental Design, and Reliability Engineering.

item.title: QE ArXiv Watch: Week of February 09 - February 16, 2026
item.description:
The quiet theme this week: design is really about acknowledging correlation. Whether we’re placing sensors in a PDE, choosing which network nodes to “poke,” or deciding if an LLM actually got worse, the most useful papers are the ones that stop pretending measurements are independent and start exploiting the paired structure we actually have.

Sensor placement, but make it infinite-dimensional (and batch-friendly)

Mäkinen et al. take Bayesian A-optimal design (minimize posterior variance) into the continuous domain where “candidate locations” aren’t a tidy list—they’re a continuum. The clever move is a convex relaxation: instead of picking k discrete sensors, we pick a measure over the domain with fixed total mass. That sounds abstract, but it buys you real math: they prove the relaxed expected A-optimal utility is concave in the design measure, so any local maximizer is global. For practitioners, that’s the difference between “hope your optimizer behaved” and “you can trust the hill you climbed is the hill.”

Then they do the next practical thing: batch constraints. In the real world, we place multiple sensors at once, and we don’t want all particles collapsing onto the same spot. Their tensorized/product-measure formulation plus two regularizers—one to concentrate each ensemble to a point, and an MMD repulsion to keep ensembles distinct—feels like a principled version of “don’t give me four sensors all at x=0.5.” If you’ve ever had a Bayesian design method hand you duplicates, you’ll recognize the pain.

One detail worth internalizing: representing a batch as an empirical measure μ_B=(1/B)∑ δ_{x_j} corresponds to independent observations but with noise scaled by √(B). That interpretation helps keep you honest about what your “relaxation” is really assuming when you translate it back to data.

Controllability scoring is secretly OED (and the invariance matters)

Sato et al. connect two communities that often talk past each other: network controllability scoring and optimal experimental design. Under a node-wise “virtual actuation” model, the finite-time controllability Gramian becomes an affine sum over nodes—structurally the same object as an OED information matrix. The mapping is clean: volumetric controllability score ↔ D-optimality, average-energy controllability score ↔ A-optimality.

The punchline is the invariance gap. The D-optimal analogue is invariant under nonsingular coordinate changes (good—physics doesn’t depend on your basis), while the A-optimal analogue generally isn’t. If you use AECS/A-optimal-like scores on a state that’s been scaled or transformed, you may literally be optimizing a different problem. That’s not a minor nuance; it’s a “check your units and coordinate choices before you ship a conclusion” warning.

They also point out something DOE folks will find interesting: approximate OED often has non-unique optimizers, whereas controllability-score optima are often unique (almost everywhere in horizon length). That uniqueness could be a feature if you need stable decisions, but it can also hide modeling assumptions that OED would force you to confront.

A real experiment designed to discriminate models (plasma heat conduction)

Vincent et al. are doing “DOE” in the original, physical sense: iterating a laser target/beam configuration until the resulting diagnostics can actually separate competing heat-conduction models in magnetized, weakly collisional plasma. The simulations suggest temperature evolution around 2.0–2.5 ns is highly discriminative (Spitzer vs suppressed vs off), while magnetic-field magnitudes are less so—useful guidance if you’re prioritizing diagnostic bandwidth.

The engineering takeaway: they don’t just simulate the physics; they simulate the measurement process (x-ray ratio thermometry, spectroscopy, proton radiography) to ask: “Will we be able to tell?” That mindset generalizes nicely to any expensive test campaign.

Reliability for model releases: paired tests, not wishful thinking

Kübler et al. bring a much-needed statistical spine to “did our optimized LLM degrade?” If baseline and optimized models are evaluated on the same benchmark items, the outcomes are paired, and naive independent-SE comparisons waste power and misstate uncertainty. Their answer is an exact one-sided McNemar/binomial test on disagreement counts, plus sensible aggregation across benchmarks (pooled, max-drop via Monte Carlo, Fisher). The result: they can flag degradations on the order of 0.3–0.8% as significant—exactly the regime where product teams argue endlessly.

One practical gem: trimming “never-flip” items can nearly halve evaluation cost while increasing sensitivity, because you’re focusing on items near the decision boundary.

Forward look

Across domains, we’re seeing the same move: stop treating evaluation as IID and start designing around the dependencies (paired outcomes, shared priors, coupled sensors, coordinated batches). The question to sit with: in your current DOE/reliability workflow, where are you still paying an “independence tax” out of habit—and what would you gain by modeling the pairing explicitly?

Featured Papers This Week

  • Mäkinen et al.: Batch-based Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design in Linear Inverse Problems (Experimental Design)
  • Sato et al.: Relationship Between Controllability Scoring and Optimal Experimental Design (Experimental Design)
  • Zhang et al.: The Effect of Design Thinking on Creative & Innovation Processes: An Empirical Study Across Different Design Experience Levels (Experimental Design)
  • Vincent et al.: Design of experiments characterising heat conduction in magnetised, weakly collisional plasma (Experimental Design)
  • Kübler et al.: When LLMs get significantly worse: A statistical approach to detect model degradations (Reliability)

Explore More: Visit the QE ArXiv Watch Dashboard to browse all papers with AI summaries, interactive filtering, and paper chat.

This digest is automatically generated every Monday. Questions or feedback? Open an issue on our GitHub repository.
